title: Dog Nail Bed Swollen With Pus: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's nail bed swollen or leaking pus? Learn about paronychia, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Swelling and pus in a dog's nail bed indicates a painful infection called paronychia that requires veterinary attention within 24 to 48 hours to prevent the infection from spreading to the bone.

What causes swelling and pus in my dog's nail bed?

When a dog's nail bed is swollen and discharging pus, it is typically a condition known as paronychia. This is an inflammatory infection of the soft tissue surrounding the claw. Common causes include:

  • Bacterial or fungal infections
  • Minor puncture wounds
  • A broken or fractured nail
  • Chronic moisture from excessive licking due to allergies

Is a swollen and discharging nail bed considered an emergency for my dog?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While a swollen nail bed is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is highly painful and indicates an active infection. To ensure proper recovery, you should:

  • Seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Avoid delaying treatment to prevent the infection from spreading to the digital bone (osteomyelitis).
  • Take action early to avoid the need for aggressive treatment or toe amputation.

How does providing a photo of the affected paw help with my dog's triage?

Providing a high-quality photo of the affected paw is incredibly helpful for remote triage. A veterinarian or technician can examine the image to:

  • Check for signs of a foreign body
  • Assess the angle of a potential nail fracture
  • Evaluate the extent of the redness and inflammation
  • Determine if your pet needs an immediate appointment or if the situation can wait until a scheduled slot the following day

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Puncture wounds of the foot are a common cause of subsolar sepsis in animals. A puncture wound to the nail bed can introduce pathogenic microorganisms, leading to subsolar abscess formation characterized by swelling and pus. If a tract is found leading to a subsolar abscess, adequate drainage should be established, keeping the drainage hole small to avoid prolapse of sensitive corium. In cases of suspected abscess without a visible tract, poulticing the foot can promote abscess localization. Puncture wounds near synovial structures require rapid, aggressive diagnosis and therapy, including broad-spectrum antibiotics.

Chapter: Integument, Orthopedics, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1115)

faqs.question: Can I drain the pus at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to squeeze or drain an infected nail bed at home. This can cause the infection to push deeper into the tissue and causes significant pain to your dog.

faqs.question: What can I do for my dog while waiting for the vet?
faqs.answer: You can perform a gentle soak using warm water and unscented Epsom salts for 5-10 minutes to help soothe the area. Ensure you dry the paw thoroughly afterward and prevent your dog from licking the site.

faqs.question: Will my dog need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: In most cases where pus is present, a veterinarian will prescribe a course of oral antibiotics or antifungal medication, and potentially anti-inflammatory drugs to manage the pain and swelling.
